tagline: Determine the correct circuit breaker size per NEC load rules
title: Breaker Sizing Calculator — NEC 80% Rule & Standard Sizes
metaDescription: Calculate the right breaker size for any load. Applies NEC 80% rule for continuous loads and recommends standard breaker and wire sizes.
keywords: breaker size calculator, circuit breaker sizing, NEC 80% rule, what size breaker do I need, breaker calculator, electrical panel sizing
calculatorHTML:
Breaker Sizing Calculator

Continuous loads require 125% sizing per NEC 210.20(A)

howItWorks.intro: Breaker sizing is about matching protection to the wire and load. The breaker protects the wire, not the appliance — so it must be sized correctly for both the load type and conductor ampacity.
rules: Continuous loads (3+ hours): breaker must be rated for 125% of the load current per NEC 210.20(A). A 16A continuous load needs a 20A breaker minimum
Non-continuous loads: breaker must be rated at or above the actual load current. Use the next standard size up
Motor loads: size at 125% of the motor's full-load amps per NEC 430.52, then select the next standard breaker size
howItWorks.outro: Standard residential breaker sizes are 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, and 100 amps. The wire gauge must match or exceed the breaker rating — never put a 20A breaker on 14 AWG wire.

faq.question: What is the NEC 80% rule for breakers?
faq.answer: The NEC 80% rule means continuous loads (running 3+ hours) can only use 80% of a breaker's rating. A 20A breaker can handle only 16A continuously. This is really a 125% sizing rule — multiply the continuous load by 1.25 to find the minimum breaker size.

faq.question: What size breaker do I need for a 240V water heater?
faq.answer: A typical 4,500W water heater at 240V draws 18.75A. Since it's a continuous load, multiply by 1.25 = 23.4A minimum. The next standard breaker size is 25A or 30A. Most installations use a 30A double-pole breaker with 10 AWG wire.

faq.question: Can I use a bigger breaker if my circuit keeps tripping?
faq.answer: Never upsize a breaker without also upsizing the wire. The breaker protects the wire from overheating. If a 15A breaker trips, the fix is to reduce the load on the circuit or run a new circuit — not install a 20A breaker on 14 AWG wire, which creates a fire hazard.

faq.question: What size wire goes with each breaker size?
faq.answer: Common pairings (copper THHN): 15A breaker = 14 AWG, 20A = 12 AWG, 30A = 10 AWG, 40A = 8 AWG, 50A = 6 AWG, 60A = 6 AWG, 100A = 3 AWG. These are minimums — always check voltage drop for long runs.

faq.question: How do I size a breaker for a motor?
faq.answer: Motor circuits are special under NEC Article 430. Size the breaker at 125% of the motor's full-load amps (from NEC Table 430.248, not the nameplate). Use the next standard size up. Motor branch circuits also need overload protection sized differently from the breaker.

faq.question: What is the difference between single-pole and double-pole breakers?
faq.answer: Single-pole breakers provide 120V and take one panel slot. Double-pole breakers provide 240V and take two slots. For 240V loads like dryers, ranges, water heaters, and AC units, you need a double-pole breaker. Some 120V circuits use double-pole for MWBC (multi-wire branch circuits).
